name: Tessman Farm
short_description: Private ranch rockhounding site in South Texas known for agate and chalcedony specimens. Flat terrain ideal for family collectors seeking colorful nodules and cut-stone material.
description:
Tessman Farm is a private rockhounding location in Karnes County, South Texas, where collectors can search for agate, chalcedony, quartz, and jasper specimens. The property sits at only 420 feet elevation on flat, easily accessible terrain typical of the South Texas brushland. This region's geological heritage includes Tertiary volcanic activity and sedimentary deposits that created prolific agate and chalcedony formations throughout the area. The site appeals to both beginners and experienced rockhounds seeking quality specimens suitable for display, cutting, and polishing.

The landscape here is characteristic of the Texas Coastal Bend - relatively flat grassland with scattered brush and cacti. Walking distances are manageable, and the lack of elevation change makes this an excellent choice for families with children or collectors with limited mobility. Agate nodules are found in the soil and weathered outcrops, often with beautiful banding patterns and translucent qualities.

Access to Tessman Farm requires prior arrangement with the landowner. Because this is private land, collection rules and permissions must be confirmed before any visit. Many private Texas ranches operate on a verbal or written agreement basis, so clear communication about your collecting intentions is essential.

The South Texas agate field has a strong rockhounding tradition dating back decades. Collectors throughout Texas and beyond seek out the region's distinctive fire agate, blue chalcedony, and banded agate specimens. Tessman Farm continues this legacy as one of several private sites supporting the local rockhounding community.
history: Tessman Farm sits within the South Texas agate belt, a region shaped by Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary activity roughly 20 to 60 million years ago. Silica-rich groundwater percolated through host rocks and filled cavities and fractures, creating the agate nodules and chalcedony crusts found here today. This area has been a documented rockhounding destination since the mid-20th century.
tips: Contact the landowner well in advance to arrange access and confirm current collection rules. Search the weathered slopes and wash areas where erosion exposes agate nodules - look for translucent or banded specimens with reddish, brown, or blue hues. Bring a geology hammer and chisel for extracting nodules; a 5-gallon bucket works well for transport on flat terrain. Early morning visits are most productive before heat builds and visibility decreases.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain at 420 feet elevation typical of South Texas brushland. Grassland with scattered low brush, cacti, and occasional small outcrops. No significant elevation changes. Walking distances from parking area are moderate and manageable for most fitness levels. Ground surface is firm soil and grass with scattered rocks. Footing is generally stable but can be uneven in places.
best_season: October - April. South Texas experiences intense summer heat (regularly exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit June - September), making outdoor rockhounding uncomfortable and potentially dangerous. Cooler, drier fall and winter months provide ideal conditions for extended collecting with minimal heat hazard.
written_directions: From Deweesville, take TX-44 south approximately 3 miles toward Mathis. Watch for ranch entrances on either side of the highway. Tessman Farm is accessible via private ranch road (exact entry point and gate location should be confirmed with the landowner before visiting). Standard passenger vehicles can access the property if ground conditions permit; contact ahead for current road status.
road_conditions: Private ranch road conditions vary seasonally; contact owner for current status before visiting. Generally passable by standard passenger vehicles in dry conditions.
parking_notes: Parking arrangements depend on landowner agreement. Typically informal pullout or parking area near ranch entrance or collecting zone.
collection_rules: Private land - rockhounding only by written or verbal permission from landowner. Collection rules, hours, and access dates must be confirmed directly with the property owner before any visit. No commercial collection or resale of material. Respect property boundaries and leave gates as found. All trash must be packed out.
rules: Permission from landowner required before accessing property. Respect all property boundaries and fencing. Pack out all trash and collected materials. Leave gates as found. No commercial collection. Confirm access rules and collecting guidelines with the owner for current terms.
nearest_services: Deweesville, Texas (approximately 3 miles north) has basic services including gas stations and small restaurants. Corpus Christi (approximately 40 miles southeast) offers full services including hospitals and equipment suppliers.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain with minimal elevation change makes this site accessible to most collectors including families with young children and those with limited mobility. No steep slopes or climbing required. Ground is firm grassland with stable footing. Distances from parking are short and manageable. Sturdy walking shoes recommended for comfort, but this is not a technical or hazardous hike.

faq.question: What types of agates and gemstones can I find at Tessman Farm near Deweesville?
faq.answer: Tessman Farm is known for agate, chalcedony, quartz, and jasper specimens. The site produces colorful banded agates and translucent chalcedony nodules typical of the South Texas agate belt. Many specimens are suitable for display, cutting, and polishing.

faq.question: How do I get permission to rockhound at Tessman Farm in Texas?
faq.answer: Tessman Farm is private land requiring landowner permission before visiting. Contact the property owner in advance to arrange access and confirm current collecting rules and availability. Written or verbal agreement must be established before you visit.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Tessman Farm?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for collecting at Tessman Farm. South Texas summer temperatures regularly exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit, making outdoor rockhounding uncomfortable and potentially dangerous. Fall and winter provide cooler, more pleasant conditions for extended collecting.

faq.question: Is Tessman Farm rockhounding site family-friendly and suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Tessman Farm is excellent for families and beginner rockhounds. The flat terrain at only 420 feet elevation requires no hiking or climbing. Short walking distances and stable ground make it accessible to children and collectors of all experience levels. Agate nodules are relatively easy to locate and extract.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Tessman Farm?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, chisel, and pry bar for extracting agate nodules from the ground. A 5-gallon bucket is useful for carrying specimens on the flat terrain. Wear sturdy walking shoes, sun protection, and bring water. Since summer heat is extreme, collect during the cooler October - April season when possible.

faq.question: How far is Tessman Farm from Deweesville and what are nearby services?
faq.answer: Tessman Farm is approximately 3 miles south of Deweesville, Texas, accessible via TX-44. Deweesville offers basic services including gas and food. Corpus Christi (40 miles southeast) has comprehensive services including hospitals, equipment suppliers, and restaurants.
